A Rule-Based Gym Workout Recommendation Expert System

Description of Proposed Project

The goal of this project is to create a rule-based expert system that suggests gym workout schedules according to the unique characteristics of each user. Having organized and efficient workouts is difficult for many gym users, particularly beginners and university students. They often copy other people's routines and rely on random internet advice without considering whether the plans are appropriate for their time constraints, experience level, or fitness objectives. This may result in inefficient training, slow progress, or avoidable injuries. The goal of this project is to develop an intelligent system that uses explicit expert knowledge encoded as rules to mimic a fitness trainer's reasoning process, which is a well-established approach in classical expert systems [1], [2].

The problem domain addressed by this system is personal fitness training in a gym environment. The system focuses on workout planning rather than medical diagnosis or advanced sports science analysis. It is intended to assist users in selecting suitable training structures for common fitness goals such as fat loss, muscle gain, and endurance improvement. The system will interact with the user through a graphical user interface, where the user answers a set of questions similar to those asked during an initial consultation with a personal trainer. These inputs will include the user’s fitness goal, experience level, number of training days per week, available session duration, and whether the user has any injuries or physical limitations.

After collecting the necessary inputs, the expert system will apply its rule base to generate a structured workout recommendation. The output will include a suggested workout split, overall training focus, and an appropriate intensity level. If the user indicates an injury, the system will also provide safety advice and recommend lower-risk alternatives based on established exercise guidelines [3], [4]. In addition to the recommendation itself, the system will present a short explanation describing why the decision was made. This explanation is an essential feature of expert systems because it allows the user to understand the reasoning behind the recommendation and increases trust in the system’s conclusions [1].

For example, a beginner user whose goal is fat loss and who can train three days per week may receive a recommendation for a full-body workout routine combined with moderate cardiovascular training. This aligns with standard exercise prescription principles, which recommend full-body resistance training for beginners to promote balanced development and energy expenditure [3]. In contrast, an advanced user training five days per week with a muscle gain goal may receive a recommendation for a split routine with higher intensity resistance training, consistent with established strength and hypertrophy guidelines [4].

Although many fitness applications exist, most rely on fixed templates or generic plans. These systems usually do not explain how their recommendations are generated and therefore do not fully meet the characteristics of an expert system. The proposed project focuses on transparent, rule-based reasoning, where each recommendation can be traced back to specific rules in the knowledge base. This structure follows the classical architecture of knowledge-based systems described in artificial intelligence literature [1], [5]. The main goal of the project is to produce consistent and logically justified workout recommendations that align with established fitness principles. The system will be considered successful if it generates appropriate recommendations and explanations for a set of predefined test cases.

Possible AI Methods and Tools

The proposed system will use a rule-based expert system approach. This method is appropriate for problems where decisions are based on structured expert knowledge rather than large datasets. Rule-based systems use IF–THEN production rules and an inference engine to derive conclusions from known facts [1], [6]. Workout planning is typically guided by established training principles and trainer experience, which can be effectively represented using such production rules. For example, a rule may state that if a user is a beginner with a fat loss goal and limited training days, a full-body routine should be recommended. Another rule may adjust the intensity or exercise selection if the user reports an injury. The system will use a forward chaining inference mechanism, where conclusions are reached by matching user inputs with relevant rules in the knowledge base [6].

The knowledge base will consist of approximately thirty to forty rules derived from general fitness training principles and recognised fitness guidelines such as those provided by the American College of Sports Medicine [3]. These rules will represent common decision patterns used by fitness trainers, such as selecting workout structures based on training frequency or modifying intensity levels according to experience level [4].

The backend of the system will be implemented using the Python programming language, which is well suited for building rule-based logic and inference mechanisms. The frontend graphical user interface will be developed using React, a widely used JavaScript library for building interactive user interfaces [7]. The React interface will collect user inputs and send them to the Python backend through a simple web API. The backend will process the inputs using the expert system and return the recommendation and explanation to the frontend. All tools used in this project are free, legal, and appropriate for academic use.

Evaluation Method

The system will be evaluated using a test-case based validation approach, which is commonly used for expert systems to verify correctness and rule consistency [1], [5]. A set of representative user profiles will be created, covering typical combinations of fitness goals, experience levels, and training availability. For each test case, the system’s output will be compared with an expected recommendation based on established fitness guidelines [3], [4]. This comparison will allow the correctness of the system’s reasoning to be assessed.

In addition to correctness, the system will be evaluated for consistency. Identical inputs should always produce identical outputs, which is a key requirement for rule-based expert systems [6]. The clarity and usefulness of the explanation provided by the system will also be considered during evaluation. A system that provides correct recommendations but unclear reasoning would be less effective than one that explains its decisions clearly and logically.

Due to time and ethical constraints, user satisfaction studies involving real participants will not be conducted. Ideally, evaluation for this type of system would involve feedback from certified fitness trainers or controlled user studies. However, such evaluations would require ethics approval and additional time. For this project, correctness, consistency, and explanation quality will be used as the primary evaluation criteria.
